Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We need to generate a DuckDB SQL query based on the prompt. The user asks: Consider an event log stored in a DuckDB table named event_log with columns case_id activity timestamp. A process variant is defined as the ordered sequence of activities that occur in a single case determined by sorting events within that case by timestamp. Task 1 Construct a DuckDB SQL query that determines the sequence of activities for each case_id maintaining the order imposed by timestamp 2 Group cases by their complete activity sequence to identify unique process variants 3 Count how many cases correspond to each variant and determine the top K variants by their frequency 4 Return all events from the original event_log that belong only to cases which are part of these top K variants

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: The prompt doesnt specify what K is but it says top K variants. I can either parameterize it or assume a fixed K e.g. 3

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Ill use DuckDB SQL syntax. Ill use string_agg to create a concatenated sequence for variant identification Could also use LIST(activity ORDER BY timestamp). DuckDB supports LIST() with ORDER BY. Then group by this list to count cases

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then get top K variants by count descending. I need to handle ties The prompt says top K variants by their frequency typically uses rank or row_number may need to handle ties if you want exactly K variants. Its ambiguous. Usually top K means the K variants with highest counts using count descending limit K. But if there are ties for the Kth theyd be excluded unless using WITH TIES clause. DuckDB doesnt support FETCH WITH TIES natively in some versions In latest DuckDB you can use QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY count DESC) <= K or QUALIFY DENSE_RANK() OVER () <= K to include ties. Ill decide to use DENSE_RANK to include ties if ranks are equal to be safe assuming that top K variants might mean the K highest frequency groups inclusive of ties

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Outline - With case_sequences as (select case_id list(activity order by timestamp) as variant_seq from event_log group by case_id) - With variant_counts as (select variant_seq count(*) as case_count from case_sequences group by variant_seq) - With top_variants as (select variant_seq case_count from (select * dense_rank() over (order by case_count desc) as rnk from variant_counts) where rnk <= K) - Then final select e.* from event_log e inner join top_variants t on e.case_id in (select cs.case_id from case_sequences cs where cs.variant_seq = t.variant_seq)

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Alternative simpler compute variant_seq list per case then compute counts rank then filter to top K variants then get all events where case_id has a variant_seq equal to one of those top variant sequences. So final join. first get eligible case_ids by joining case_sequences with top_variants (select cs.case_id from case_sequences cs join top_variants tv on cs.variant_seq = tv.variant_seq). Then join event_log

Name: Validation - PE
Text: DuckDB supports list(activity ORDER BY timestamp). Yes. Ill write query with a parameter K as a literal or named parameter. Ill use 3 for illustration. Ill mention how to adjust
